questions.question: Why does carbon form compounds mainly by covalent bonding?
questions.answer: Carbon has 4 electrons in its valence shell. It cannot lose 4 electrons (requires too much energy) or gain 4 electrons (difficult for 6 protons to hold 10 electrons). Therefore, it shares electrons to achieve stability.

questions.question: Explain the nature of the covalent bond using the bond formation in $CH_4$.
questions.answer: In methane, one carbon atom shares its 4 valence electrons with 4 hydrogen atoms (each sharing 1 electron). This results in 4 single covalent bonds, allowing all atoms to achieve noble gas configuration.

questions.question: What are the two properties of carbon which lead to the huge number of carbon compounds?
questions.answer: 1. Catenation: The ability to form bonds with other carbon atoms. 2. Tetravalency: The ability to bond with four other atoms.

questions.question: Saturated hydrocarbons burn with a blue flame while unsaturated ones burn with a yellow sooty flame. Why?
questions.answer: Saturated hydrocarbons undergo complete combustion due to high carbon-hydrogen ratio. Unsaturated ones have more carbon, leading to incomplete combustion and unburnt carbon particles (soot).

questions.question: What is a homologous series? State any two characteristics.
questions.answer: A series of compounds with the same functional group and chemical properties. Characteristics: 1. Members differ by a $-CH_2$ group. 2. Members differ by 14u in molecular mass.

questions.question: How can ethanol and ethanoic acid be differentiated on the basis of their physical and chemical properties?
questions.answer: Physical: Ethanol has a pleasant smell; Ethanoic acid smells like vinegar. Chemical: Ethanoic acid reacts with sodium bicarbonate to evolve $CO_2$ gas (brisk effervescence), whereas ethanol does not.

questions.question: What is hydrogenation? What is its industrial application?
questions.answer: Hydrogenation is the addition of hydrogen to unsaturated hydrocarbons in the presence of catalysts like Nickel. Industrial application: Conversion of vegetable oils (unsaturated) into vegetable ghee (saturated).

questions.question: Why is the conversion of ethanol to ethanoic acid an oxidation reaction?
questions.answer: Because it involves the addition of oxygen and the removal of hydrogen from the ethanol molecule, usually facilitated by alkaline $KMnO_4$ or acidified $K_2Cr_2O_7$.

questions.question: What is an esterification reaction? Write the equation.
questions.answer: The reaction of a carboxylic acid with an alcohol in the presence of an acid catalyst to form an ester. $CH_3COOH + C_2H_5OH \xrightarrow{Acid} CH_3COOC_2H_5 + H_2O$.

questions.question: What is saponification? Why is it called so?
questions.answer: It is the alkaline hydrolysis of an ester to give back the alcohol and the sodium salt of carboxylic acid. It is used in the preparation of soaps.

questions.question: Explain the cleansing action of soap with the help of a diagram description.
questions.answer: Soap molecules have a hydrophilic head and a hydrophobic tail. The tails attach to dirt/oil, while heads stay in water, forming a structure called a micelle that pulls dirt away.

questions.question: Why does soap form scum with hard water while detergents do not?
questions.answer: Soap reacts with Calcium and Magnesium ions in hard water to form insoluble precipitates (scum). Detergents do not form insoluble salts with these ions.

questions.question: Draw the electron dot structure for ethyne ($C_2H_2$).
questions.answer: Ethyne has a triple bond between the two carbon atoms ($H-C \equiv C-H$). Each Carbon shares 3 electrons with the other Carbon and 1 with Hydrogen.

questions.question: What are isomers? Draw the structures of two isomers of butane.
questions.answer: Compounds with the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements. Butane ($C_4H_{10}$) has n-butane (straight chain) and isobutane (branched chain).

questions.question: What will be the formula and electron dot structure of cyclopentane?
questions.answer: Formula: $C_5H_{10}$. It forms a closed ring of 5 carbon atoms, each bonded to 2 hydrogen atoms.

questions.question: Name the functional groups present in: (i) $CH_3COCH_3$ (ii) $CH_3CHO$.
questions.answer: (i) Ketone ($-CO-$) (ii) Aldehyde ($-CHO$).

questions.question: What is 'denatured alcohol'?
questions.answer: Ethanol to which poisonous substances like methanol or dyes (copper sulphate) are added to make it unfit for drinking and prevent its industrial misuse.

questions.question: Why are coal and petroleum called fossil fuels?
questions.answer: Because they were formed from the biomass of plants and animals that lived millions of years ago and were buried under the earth.

questions.question: What is a catalyst? Give an example of its use in carbon compounds.
questions.answer: A substance that changes the rate of a reaction without being consumed. Example: Nickel or Palladium used in the hydrogenation of oils.

questions.question: Why is pure ethanoic acid called glacial acetic acid?
questions.answer: Because it has a melting point of 290K and often freezes during winter in cold climates, looking like a glacier.

questions.question: Write the chemical equation for the reaction of ethanoic acid with Sodium Carbonate.
questions.answer: $2CH_3COOH + Na_2CO_3 \rightarrow 2CH_3COONa + H_2O + CO_2$.

questions.question: Compare the structure of Diamond and Graphite.
questions.answer: In Diamond, each carbon is bonded to 4 others in a 3D structure (hard). In Graphite, each carbon is bonded to 3 others in layers (soft, conducts electricity).

questions.question: What is the role of concentrated sulphuric acid in the esterification reaction?
questions.answer: It acts as both a catalyst and a dehydrating agent, helping to remove the water formed and shifting the equilibrium to produce more ester.

questions.question: Name the reaction: $CH_4 + Cl_2 \xrightarrow{Sunlight} CH_3Cl + HCl$.
questions.answer: Substitution reaction.

questions.question: What are buckminsterfullerenes?
questions.answer: An allotrope of carbon where carbon atoms are arranged in the shape of a football. The first identified was C-60.
